text: Hi guys, this is Dr. Andrew Iraniha from Surgical Oasis Institute. This video demonstrates my technique for robotic bilateral inguinal hernia repair with mesh. This is a thirty five year old gentleman who presented with bilateral groin bulge and on and off pain and discomfort and found to have evidence of bilateral inguinal hernia. He was then taken to the operating room for robotic assisted bilateral inguinal hernia repair with mesh.

The surgeon places three small incisions in the upper abdomen and inserts robotic instruments and a scope, connecting them to the robotic arm. A transverse incision is made in the peritoneum, revealing the smooth inner layer of the abdomen. The surgeon creates a pocket in the preperitoneal space, precisely dissecting and separating critical structures with minimal bleeding. Testicular vessels are carefully separated from the peritoneum, and incarcerated fat is evaluated and excised from the hernia defect visible in the right upper side of the field.

The surgeon uses cautery and scissors to perform dissection in the left groin region, creating a pocket for mesh placement. The epigastric vessels are visible at the top of the field, serving as a landmark. The surgeon separates the testicular vessels from the peritoneum and identifies the vas deferens as a bright structure on the right lower side. Fat stuck in the hernia defect is mobilized and removed, revealing the hernia on the right upper side of the field. The preperitoneal pocket is completed, and the mesh is placed.

The surgeon changes instruments and enlarges the preperitoneal pocket, placing the mesh and unfolding it like a carpet behind the muscle. The mesh is precisely positioned to prevent movement and sits smoothly without wrinkles. It is tucked underneath the peritoneum to cover it from intra-abdominal structures. A non-absorbable suture is used to secure the mesh inferiorly to Cooper's ligament, ensuring stability.

The surgeon applies bio glue to secure the mesh to the abdominal wall and reduce postoperative bleeding. The bio glue is absorbable and temporarily adheres the mesh, peritoneum, and muscle until scar tissue forms. The surgeon then closes the peritoneal defect in two layers to ensure the mesh is completely buried behind the muscle, preventing exposure to intra-abdominal structures.

Minimally invasive surgery including robotic or laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair allows the surgeon to perform this complex operation through the small incision and therefore decrease the trauma to the abdominal wall and patient which actually translates into the faster recovery and less pain after the surgery. This operation took one hour and ten minutes. It was done as an outpatient. The patient went home the same day. After a couple days he stopped taking pain medication and he was very comfortable to go back to work a few days later. And he resumed his heavy exercise after six weeks without any problem.

The surgeon closes a small peritoneal defect to ensure the mesh is not exposed to intra-abdominal structures. Using a laparoscopic technique, the surgeon removes the needle and excised pieces of fat that were previously incarcerated in the hernia defect.
If you like this video, please follow me at my YouTube channel Surgical Oasis Institute or for consultation call my office, nine four nine six four six eight four four four. Thank you.
